feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: Hands-on Tech Leader | Systematic Trading | Market Data | Agentic AI

entry.title: AI for the Manager Running the Team

link.title: AI for the Manager Running the Team

entry.content:
Everyone’s talking about AI for coding. Nobody’s talking about what it does for the manager running the team.

Yes, AI makes you a better engineer. But here’s where it’s moved the needle across the full stack of engineering leadership:

Sprint planning used to eat half the day. Now: jot down sparks, map them to tickets, generate entire ticket sets wholesale. It detects dependencies, flags priority cycles, and when you’re up against a time constraint it helps cut scope intelligently — not arbitrarily. Connected via MCP to search, update, assign, and reprioritize without ever leaving the session.

Stakeholder reporting used to mean manually pulling from a dozen places. Now: scrape 20+ tickets, define criteria, get a report shaped for whoever’s reading it. End-of-day summaries just happen.

Architecture and security reviews — first-pass reviews before committee. Database tradeoffs, stored proc design, materialization decisions — show up prepared without being the DBA or the security engineer.

Infrastructure — Terraform, Kubernetes, ClickHouse, GitHub webhooks, DB performance. The bottleneck was never intelligence, it was syntax and context-switching. That’s gone.

Legacy systems — documenting undocumented codebases, mapping dependencies, generating architecture diagrams. If you’ve inherited one, you know what this is worth.

Test cases, refactoring, UI mockups before engineering starts. All of it.

A note to engineers: hook your troubleshooting sessions into your Linear or Jira tickets. Capture findings, reasoning, execution plan as you go. Your ticket becomes a living record. “What did you do today?” answers itself. Documentation stops being overhead — it becomes a byproduct of how you work.

Something not enough people are talking about: explore what MCPs you actually have access to. Most people haven’t looked. MCP connects your AI to your real tools — Linear, GitHub, Slack, your databases — natively. Build a handful of workflows you trigger on the flick of a finger and run every single day.

The real benefit isn’t speed. It’s communication — richer updates, preserved context, nothing lost. When EOY review comes around you have a trail. A story. Evidence.

For engineers: “what did you do this quarter?” answered with receipts. For managers: “what did your team deliver?” answered with confidence. For introverts who do the deepest work but hate self-promoting — it speaks for you. Quietly. Continuously. Without performing.

Read the full post on LinkedIn

Original post with comments and discussion.

View on LinkedIn →
entry.summary: Beyond coding: how AI and MCP workflows move the needle across sprint planning, stakeholder reporting, architecture reviews, and self-documentation.

entry.title: The AIops Playbook: Because ‘It Works’ Isn’t a Strategy

link.title: The AIops Playbook: Because ‘It Works’ Isn’t a Strategy

entry.content:
Teams building on AI platforms often lack visibility into performance degradation — and every major provider has had significant outages, quality regressions, or unexplained model drift in the past year. Stanford research documented GPT-4 accuracy dropping from 97% to 2% on identical tasks. You can’t optimize what you can’t see.

Six practices that help: token budgeting, model tiering, prompt versioning, evaluation pipelines, sunset planning, and token-level observability per workflow.

Read the full post on LinkedIn

Full playbook with reasoning behind each practice.

View on LinkedIn →
entry.summary: Six operational practices for AI workflows — token budgeting, model tiering, prompt versioning, eval pipelines, sunset planning, and token-level observability.

entry.title: Reviving a 2015 FPGA Board with Claude Code

link.title: Reviving a 2015 FPGA Board with Claude Code

entry.content:
Built and deployed 10 bare-metal projects on a Digilent Zybo (Zynq-7000) in a single Claude Code session — AXI GPIO, DMA, XADC, audio, Ethernet, SD/QSPI boot. The real value was debugging: tracing incomplete Vivado automation, Vitis 2024.2 interrupt ID encoding changes, and Bank 35 I/O conflicts.

Read the full post on LinkedIn

Detailed walkthrough of every debug, fix, and lesson learned.

View on LinkedIn →
entry.summary: 10 bare-metal FPGA projects in one session on a Zynq-7000 — debugging DMA, interrupts, and XADC with Claude Code as a hardware-literate partner.

entry.title: What If LLMs Don’t Need to Reason?

link.title: What If LLMs Don’t Need to Reason?

entry.content:
What if LLMs don’t need to reason? What if they just need to translate and rank — and the rest is orchestration over tools that already exist? Sketched an architecture exploring how most everyday reasoning tasks may not require sophisticated reasoning models at all.

Read the full post on LinkedIn

Architecture sketch and discussion on translation-and-ranking vs. reasoning.

View on LinkedIn →
entry.summary: A sketch of an architecture where LLMs translate intent and rank solutions — with external tools handling the actual reasoning.
